source: MED lead magnet — 7 Red Flags at the Shop

red_flags.name: The Phone Diagnosis
pattern_keywords: gave me a price without looking
red_flags.description: The shop quoted a price before seeing the vehicle.
red_flags.why_problematic: A responsible shop cannot diagnose a vehicle over the phone. A noise could be a loose heat shield ($50) or a failing wheel bearing ($400+). Anyone who quotes before looking is guessing — or has already decided what they're going to sell you.
red_flags.what_to_do: A good shop will say 'Bring it in and we'll take a look.' If they quote a price before seeing it, call somewhere else.

red_flags.name: The Scare List
red_flags.description: You came in for one thing and got a long list of additional items that 'need immediate attention.'
red_flags.why_problematic: Upselling maintenance services is how some shops pad the ticket. The timing is designed to catch you off guard — you're already there, you don't want to leave and come back.
red_flags.what_to_do: Ask: 'Which of these is a safety issue and which is maintenance?' Get safety items done. Take the maintenance list home and check it against your owner's manual. You'll often find half the list isn't due yet.

red_flags.name: Open-Ended Authorization
red_flags.description: The shop can't give a firm estimate and wants authorization to start work before knowing the full cost.
red_flags.why_problematic: Once the car is disassembled, you can't easily take it somewhere else. The final bill tends to grow because you've already committed.
red_flags.what_to_do: Ask for a diagnostic fee upfront ($80-150). A good shop will diagnose, give a firm written estimate, and let you decide. If they won't give a written estimate before starting work, find a shop that will.

red_flags.name: No Old Parts
red_flags.description: They replaced parts but can't show you the old ones.
red_flags.why_problematic: Showing old parts is standard practice at honest shops. It proves the work was done and lets you see the wear. A shop that can't produce old parts may not have replaced them.
red_flags.what_to_do: Before authorizing any repair, say: 'I'd like to see the old parts when I pick up.' A legitimate shop will have no problem with this.

red_flags.name: The Pressure Close
pattern_keywords: you need to do this today
red_flags.description: Fear-based pressure to approve repairs immediately.
red_flags.why_problematic: Genuine safety issues don't require high-pressure tactics. A mechanic who respects you will explain the problem, show you what they found, and let you decide.
red_flags.what_to_do: If someone says the car is unsafe to drive, ask them to show you the problem. Get under the lift with them. Look at the worn part. If they can show it, it's probably real. If they can't or won't, it's probably a scare tactic.

red_flags.name: Mystery Charges
red_flags.description: Unexplained fees that weren't on the original estimate.
red_flags.why_problematic: Small fees add up and are designed to go unquestioned. Some are legitimate (waste disposal), but they should be disclosed upfront, not added at the register.
red_flags.what_to_do: Before authorizing work, ask: 'Is there anything on the final bill that isn't on this estimate?' If surprise charges appear, question them. You have the right to an itemized invoice that matches the authorized estimate.

red_flags.name: Resists Second Opinion
red_flags.description: The shop discourages you from getting another estimate.
red_flags.why_problematic: A shop that does good work at fair prices has no reason to discourage comparison. The resistance itself is the signal.
red_flags.what_to_do: Say: 'I appreciate the estimate. I'm going to get one more opinion before I decide.' Any shop that respects your right to do that is worth returning to. Any shop that doesn't — isn't.
